title: Inject latent degradation state features instead of only static tyre-age ratios
summary: Recent F1 research models tyre pace as a latent state with resets at pit stops, and Mercedes-associated tyre-energy forecasting work shows dynamic tyre condition is highly predictive.
rationale: Recent F1 research models tyre pace as a latent state with resets at pit stops, and Mercedes-associated tyre-energy forecasting work shows dynamic tyre condition is highly predictive. The current feature set mostly uses snapshot ratios such as TyreLife, degradation per lap, and same-lap ranks; it does not explicitly estimate whether the driver is currently entering a cliff or stabilizing.
implementation_hint: Per Driver-Race-Stint, fit a simple local-linear or Kalman-style filter on lap-time residuals after removing coarse effects from race progress, compound, and position. Export filtered level, slope, acceleration, shock from the previous lap, and state uncertainty. Mask or downweight obvious in-laps/out-laps and recent pit-stop rows when fitting the filter.
expected_effect: Should separate ‘old but stable’ tyres from ‘old and collapsing’ tyres, improving pit-window discrimination without needing a full sequential deep model.
risk: Residual estimation can be noisy because lap times contain traffic and pit artifacts; overfitting the filter parameters per race is a real danger.
disabled_reason: Disabled because an external duplicate-detection mechanism marked this hypothesis as duplicate of an existing hypothesis.
